title: Validate Binary Search Tree
problem_statement: Determine whether a binary tree is a valid binary search tree, where left subtree values are less and right subtree values are greater for every node.

examples.explanation: Every node satisfies BST ordering relative to its ancestors.

constraints: The number of nodes is in the range [1, 10^4]
intuition: Validate the BST property by carrying allowable lower and upper bounds down the tree; each node must fit within its ancestor-derived interval.

approaches.name: Bounded recursion
approaches.description: Use recursion with lower and upper bounds for each node and verify that all descendants satisfy the ancestor constraints.
approaches.why_not: Inorder traversal is another valid solution, but explicit bounds directly encode the BST invariant.
interview_commentary: Bounds ensure every node respects ancestor constraints.
The current node’s value restricts its left and right subtrees differently.
Null nodes are always valid BSTs.

approaches.name: Inorder traversal validation
approaches.description: Traverse the tree inorder and confirm the visited values are strictly increasing.
approaches.why_not: This relies on the BST property of inorder order and requires tracking the previous visited value.
interview_commentary: Inorder traversal of a BST should yield increasing values.
Track the last visited value to ensure strict ordering.
If any value is not greater than the previous, the tree is invalid.

interview_qa.q: What bounds are passed to the left subtree?
interview_qa.a: The current node's value becomes the upper bound for the left subtree.

interview_qa.q: What bounds are passed to the right subtree?
interview_qa.a: The current node's value becomes the lower bound for the right subtree.

interview_qa.q: Why is a simple parent check insufficient?
interview_qa.a: Because descendants must also satisfy all ancestor constraints, not just the immediate parent.

interview_qa.q: How does inorder traversal validate a BST?
interview_qa.a: It ensures node values are visited in strictly increasing order.
